_comment: Stage roadmap content for the personalized PDF. 4 stages, sourced from Shaykh Abdullah Oduro coaching transcripts. Text edits only — do not change field names.

1.name: The Drifter
1.tagline: Something has gone quiet inside. You know it.
1.whatThisStageIs:
You are not broken. But you are on autopilot. You wake up, you go to work, you come home, you scroll, you sleep. Maybe you pray. Maybe you provide. But your heart is not fully in any of it.

Most men live here longer than they admit. The days feel the same. The routine keeps moving. But there is a gap between who you are and who you were made to be, and you have stopped trying to close it.

This is not a failure. It is a signal. Allah does not remove purpose from a man. He only removes clarity when the man stops looking for it.

constraints.title: You have tied your worth to what you produce
constraints.body: When someone asks who you are, the first thing you say is your job title. If that job disappears tomorrow, you do not know who you are without it. Your identity has become what works, not who you are.

constraints.title: You avoid the hard questions
constraints.body: What am I actually doing with my life? Am I the man my family needs? You know these questions are there. But asking them feels heavy. So you stay busy instead. Busyness becomes the shield.

constraints.title: Your worship is mechanical
constraints.body: You pray, maybe. But you do not feel it. The salah is a motion, not a meeting. There is no muhasaba, no self-accounting. The Quran is closed more days than it is open.

constraints.title: You are carrying it alone
constraints.body: No brother knows what you are actually going through. You do not want anyone to see behind the mask. You think asking for help makes you less of a man. So you stay in your own cave, and the silence grows louder.

solutions.title: Stop and ask the real question
solutions.body: Before you fix anything, you have to be honest about where you are. Not where you tell people you are. Where you actually are. Write it down. One sentence. That sentence is the beginning.
solutions.islamicAnchor: Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves. (Qur'an 13:11)

solutions.title: Return to salah as identity, not duty
solutions.body: Prayer is not something you do. It is who you are. Start with one salah a day with full presence. No phone before it. No rushing through it. Stand there and mean it. Five minutes with Allah. That is the pitstop that resets everything.
solutions.islamicAnchor: I have not created jinn and mankind except that they worship Me. (Qur'an 51:56)

solutions.title: Find one brother who will tell you the truth
solutions.body: You do not need a crowd. You need one man who fears Allah and will not let you lie to yourself. Text him. Meet him. Tell him one thing you have been hiding. That conversation will do more than a year of scrolling at night ever will.
solutions.islamicAnchor: Each of you is a shepherd and each of you is responsible for his flock. (Bukhari & Muslim)

sevenDayPlan.day1: Pray one salah today with full presence. Just one. Put your phone in another room before you stand up. Stand there and mean it. That is your only task today.
sevenDayPlan.day2: Open the Quran and read one page. Do not worry about tajweed or speed. Just read. If you do not know where to start, open to Surah Yusuf. One page, then close it.
sevenDayPlan.day3: At the end of tonight, before you sleep, write one sentence about how you showed up as a man today. One sentence. Be honest. No one will read it but you.
sevenDayPlan.day4: Put your phone on the charger in another room after Isha. Do not pick it up until Fajr. That is it. See what happens when the screen goes dark and you are alone with your own thoughts.
sevenDayPlan.day5: Before you walk through your front door today, sit in your car for five minutes. Make dhikr. Ask Allah one thing. Take a breath. This is the pitstop. Do not bring the stress of the world into your home.
sevenDayPlan.day6: Text one brother you trust. Not small talk. Tell him: I am trying to wake up. I need someone who will not let me go back to sleep. That one message will do more than a month of doing this alone.
sevenDayPlan.day7: Pray that same salah with presence again. Read your page of Quran. Write your one sentence. Do the pitstop. You have done six days. You felt something shift. That shift is not an accident — it is Allah responding to your effort. Now build on it with brotherhood.

1.advanceCriteria: You will know you are moving toward Stage 2 when the gap starts to bother you. When missing a salah feels like something, not nothing. When you can say out loud what you are working on. When you stop hiding behind busyness and start building something real. That discomfort is not a problem. It is the wake-up.

cta.title: You are not behind. You are waking up.
cta.body: The fact that you took this assessment means something already moved inside you. That is not nothing. That is the beginning. If you want help building from here, with brothers who get it and a framework rooted in Quran and Sunnah, we are here.

2.name: The Builder
2.tagline: You can see the gap. Now close it.
2.whatThisStageIs:
You are awake. You see the distance between who you are and who you are supposed to be. That awareness is a gift from Allah. Most men never get here. They stay numb. You did not.

But awareness without consistency is just guilt. You start strong — the gym, the Quran, the early mornings — and then life pulls you back. You know what to do. You just have not built the structure to keep doing it.

This stage is not about motivation. Motivation runs out. This stage is about building the habits, the brotherhood, and the daily structure that hold you up when the feeling fades.

constraints.title: You start strong and fade
constraints.body: You have had seasons of growth. Ramadan was good. That one month you prayed Fajr every day felt different. But it did not stick. The pattern repeats: effort, progress, then a slow slide back to where you started. You need a system, not another fresh start.

constraints.title: You are doing it alone
constraints.body: You do not have brothers around you who are building toward the same thing. You might have friends, but nobody who asks you the hard questions. Nobody checking if you prayed. Nobody who will say, brother, you are slipping.

constraints.title: Your nafs knows your weak spots
constraints.body: Late at night, the phone wins. When stress hits, old habits come back. You know your patterns but you have not built real defenses against them. Shaitan whispers from the same door every time, and every time you are surprised.

constraints.title: You confuse knowing with doing
constraints.body: You have listened to the lectures. You have read the books. You know the concepts. But knowledge without action is just decoration. The man you want to be is not built from what you know. He is built from what you do when no one is watching.

solutions.title: Build one non-negotiable daily habit
solutions.body: Pick one thing and do not break it. Not five things. One. Fajr on time. Ten minutes of Quran after Fajr. A 20-minute walk. Whatever it is, make it the thing you do not negotiate with yourself on. Everything else builds on top of that one anchor.
solutions.islamicAnchor: The most beloved deeds to Allah are the most consistent, even if they are small. (Bukhari)

solutions.title: Get into a circle of men
solutions.body: You cannot build alone. The Prophet, peace be upon him, did not lead alone. Find two or three brothers who are building something in their own lives. Meet weekly. Share wins. Share failures. Hold each other. This is not optional. This is how men are forged.
solutions.islamicAnchor: Hold firmly to the rope of Allah all together and do not become divided. (Qur'an 3:103)

solutions.title: Cut one thing that is stealing your time
solutions.body: You know what it is. The WhatsApp group that gives you nothing. The late-night scroll. The hour of content that leaves you more empty than when you started. Cut one. Replace it with one action that feeds your soul. You will feel the difference in a week.
solutions.islamicAnchor: Be in this world as though you were a stranger or a wayfarer passing by. (Bukhari)

sevenDayPlan.day1: Pick your one non-negotiable. The thing you will not drop even on your worst day. Fajr on time. Ten minutes of Quran. A 20-minute walk. Write it down. That is your anchor.
sevenDayPlan.day2: Do your non-negotiable. Then identify the one thing that steals your time every night. The scroll. The group chat. The content that leaves you emptier than when you started. Name it. Tomorrow you cut it.
sevenDayPlan.day3: Do your non-negotiable. And tonight, when that time-thief calls, do not answer. Put your phone on grayscale after Isha. Replace that hour with something that feeds your soul — Quran, dhikr, or sleep.
sevenDayPlan.day4: Do your non-negotiable. Then text one brother and tell him what you are building. Not a vague update. Tell him: this is my anchor habit, and I need you to ask me about it next week. Accountability starts with one honest message.
sevenDayPlan.day5: Do your non-negotiable. Today, add muraqaba — self-monitoring. At Dhuhr, pause and ask yourself: am I on track or did I drift? One check-in during the day. That is how you catch the slide before it becomes a fall.
sevenDayPlan.day6: Do your non-negotiable. Fast today, Monday or Thursday, whichever is closer. Fasting trains the nafs like nothing else. Your body learns to say no. That discipline bleeds into everything.
sevenDayPlan.day7: Do your non-negotiable. Tonight, do muhasaba — honest self-accounting. What stuck this week? Where did you slip? Write it down. You have built something in seven days. You felt the difference. Now do not let it die — build on it with brotherhood.

2.advanceCriteria: You will know you are moving toward Stage 3 when the habits stop needing willpower. When the brothers around you can see the change before you announce it. When your wife or your family says something is different — without you telling them what you have been working on. That is the shift from building to being anchored.

cta.title: You have the awareness. Now build the structure.
cta.body: You do not need more information. You need a framework, a brotherhood, and someone who will not let you settle. That is what Masterman was built for. If you are ready to close the gap for real, let us talk.

3.name: The Anchored
3.tagline: Your foundation is real. Now face what you have been avoiding.
3.whatThisStageIs:
You have done the work most men put off. Your salah is consistent. Your provision is steady. The people around you trust you. You show up.

But there is one or two places where you have been holding back. A conversation you will not have. A role you have outgrown but will not leave. A part of yourself you keep hidden because showing it feels risky.

You are not failing. You are plateauing. And the danger of a plateau is that it feels like safety. You can stay here for years and never realize you stopped growing.

constraints.title: You have become what works, not who you are
constraints.body: You learned early what gets rewarded: providing, producing, performing. You became dependable, impressive, useful. But somewhere along the way, the man behind the function went quiet. If someone removed every role you play, who would be left standing? That question should not scare you. But it does.

constraints.title: You avoid the hard conversations at home
constraints.body: Things are fine at home. Or at least they look fine. But fine is not the same as good. There is a tension you have been managing instead of addressing. A conversation you have been putting off because the peace feels easier than the truth. Your wife may have stopped bringing it up. That does not mean it went away.

constraints.title: You mistake silence for agreement
constraints.body: Because you lead well, the people around you may have stopped telling you what is actually going on. Your children do not push back. Your wife agrees to keep the peace. You think everything is fine. But something is quietly building underneath that you cannot see because no one feels safe enough to show you.

constraints.title: Your worship is consistent but has stopped growing
constraints.body: You pray. You fast. You give. But when is the last time your salah moved you? When is the last time you cried in sujood? Consistency without depth is maintenance, not worship. The next level of your deen requires you to go deeper, not just wider.

solutions.title: Face the one thing you have been avoiding
solutions.body: You already know what it is. Name it. This week, have the conversation. Make the decision. Take the step. The thing you have been circling is the exact thing standing between you and the next version of yourself. It will not cost you as much as you think.
solutions.islamicAnchor: O you who believe, protect yourselves and your families from a fire whose fuel is people and stones. (Qur'an 66:6)

solutions.title: Ask your wife one hard question and sit with the answer
solutions.body: Do not explain. Do not defend. Ask her: what is one thing I do not see about how things are at home? Then close your mouth and listen. The strongest men are the ones who create space for honest feedback. That takes more courage than leading from the front.
solutions.islamicAnchor: And We made them leaders, guiding by Our command. (Qur'an 21:73)

solutions.title: Go deeper in worship, not just wider
solutions.body: Learn one name of Allah this week. Not just the word. What it means. Where you see it in your life. How it changes the way you pray. One name, studied deeply, will do more for your heart than a thousand rakahs done on autopilot. The scholars built libraries because they went deep. Go deep.
solutions.islamicAnchor: Indeed, we offered the trust (al-Amanah) to the heavens and the earth, and man undertook it. (Qur'an 33:72)

sevenDayPlan.day1: Name the one conversation or decision you have been avoiding. Do not fix it yet. Just write it down. Be specific. The thing you have been circling is the thing standing between you and the next version of yourself.
sevenDayPlan.day2: Learn one name of Allah today. Not just the word — what it means, where you see it in your life. Study it. Sit with it. Let it change the way you pray tonight. One name, studied deeply, will do more than a thousand rakahs on autopilot.
sevenDayPlan.day3: Do one act of worship in secret today. Something no one will know about except you and Allah. Give charity quietly. Pray two rakahs when the house is empty. This is how you train ikhlas and break the performance trap.
sevenDayPlan.day4: Ask your wife one question: what is one thing I do not see about how things are at home? Then close your mouth and listen. Do not explain. Do not defend. Just receive. That takes more courage than leading from the front.
sevenDayPlan.day5: Set the date. That conversation you wrote down on day one — decide when you will have it. This week. Put it on your calendar. The avoidance has cost you more than the conversation ever will.
sevenDayPlan.day6: Have the conversation. Make the decision. Take the step. You have been preparing for this. Trust Allah and move. Whatever comes from honesty is better than what grows from silence.
sevenDayPlan.day7: Tonight, sit and ask yourself: where am I still performing instead of leading? Where is the gap between public and private still open? You closed one gap this week. You felt the depth. Now take that depth into brotherhood — find one man to walk with, and keep going.

3.advanceCriteria: You will know you are moving toward Stage 4 when other men start coming to you — not because of your title, but because of who you are. When your wife trusts your leadership, not just your provision. When you can rest without guilt and lead without performance. When the gap between who you are in public and who you are alone has nearly closed.

cta.title: Your foundation is solid. Do not waste it on the plateau.
cta.body: Most men at Stage 3 stay here forever. Comfortable. Consistent. But never fully alive. You did not do all this work to stop here. If you are ready to go deeper with men who are at your level, we should talk.

4.name: The Shepherd
4.tagline: You are living it. Now multiply it.
4.whatThisStageIs:
Other men look to you. Not because you asked them to. Because something in you is steady, and people feel it. Your house is anchored. Your worship is real. Your identity is not contingent on what you produce.

This does not mean life is easy. It means you have learned what most men never learn: your worth comes from Allah, not from your job, your wife, your title, or your success. You carry your roles as an amana — a trust — not as the definition of who you are.

The question at this stage is not what you need to fix. It is who you are going to bring with you.

constraints.title: Strength at the top can become isolation
constraints.body: The stronger you become, the fewer men around you who will correct you. Your wife may stop pushing back. Your friends may stop challenging you. That is not respect. That is distance. A man who needs no one eventually leads no one. Brotherhood is not a luxury for men at your level. It is protection.

constraints.title: You must guard your intention above all else
constraints.body: The higher you climb, the more eyes are on you. And the more eyes on you, the harder it is to keep your actions pure. The Prophet, peace be upon him, said actions are judged by intentions. At your stage, the biggest threat is not failure. It is doing the right things for the wrong reasons.

constraints.title: You can become the function and forget the man
constraints.body: You are the provider, the leader, the one everyone leans on. But when was the last time you were just a servant of Allah — not a husband, not a father, not a professional? Just a man on his prayer mat with nothing to perform? Do not let service replace worship. Roles are tools. They are never your source of worth.

solutions.title: Do something this week that no one will know about
solutions.body: Give charity in secret. Pray in the night when the house is asleep. Help someone and do not mention it. This is the training ground for ikhlas. The Prophet, peace be upon him, said one of the seven who will be under the shade of Allah is a man who gives so secretly that his left hand does not know what his right hand has given.
solutions.islamicAnchor: The eyes shed tears and the heart is saddened, but we will not say anything that is displeasing to Allah. (Bukhari)

solutions.title: Find one man to pour into
solutions.body: Your growth now multiplies when you give it away. Find one younger man — in your family, your masjid, your community — and walk with him. Not a lecture. A relationship. Ask him questions. Listen. Share what you have learned. The legacy of a shepherd is not what he built. It is who he raised.
solutions.islamicAnchor: Whoever guides someone to good will have a reward like the one who does it. (Muslim)

solutions.title: Stay accountable to someone, always
solutions.body: One of the worst characteristics a man can have is when no one in his life can correct him. Find a man you respect — an elder, a scholar, a brother at your level — and give him permission to speak into your life without filter. That humility is not weakness. It is what separates a shepherd from a king.
solutions.islamicAnchor: And if it is said to him, fear Allah, his pride leads him to sin. (Qur'an 2:206)

sevenDayPlan.day1: Do one thing today that no one will ever know about. Give charity in secret. Pray two rakahs in the night when the house is asleep. Help someone and do not mention it. This is the training ground for ikhlas.
sevenDayPlan.day2: Identify one man in your circle who is struggling — Stage 1 or Stage 2. Not a project. A brother. Text him today. Not advice. Just: how are you doing, for real? That door you open may be the one Allah uses.
sevenDayPlan.day3: Ask your wife: where am I not seeing clearly? Listen without defending. At your level, the people around you may have stopped telling you the truth. Create the space for honesty. That is leadership.
sevenDayPlan.day4: Do another secret act of worship. Different from day one. The Prophet, peace be upon him, said one of the seven shaded on the Day of Judgment is a man who gives so secretly that his left hand does not know what his right hand has given. Train that muscle.
sevenDayPlan.day5: Sit with that brother you reached out to on day two. In person or by phone. Ask him what he is working on. Share one thing you learned the hard way. Not a lecture. A conversation. The legacy of a shepherd is not what he built. It is who he raised.
sevenDayPlan.day6: Review your roles honestly. Where are you still performing instead of being present? Where has strength become isolation? Find one man you respect — an elder, a scholar, a peer — and give him permission to speak into your life without filter. That humility is what separates a shepherd from a king.
sevenDayPlan.day7: Write one sentence answering this: what am I leaving behind? Not what you are building. What you are leaving. You have spent six days serving, pouring out, going deeper. You felt the weight of purpose. That is not something you carry alone — build on it with brotherhood.

4.advanceCriteria: There is no Stage 5. This is where you stay. But staying here is not standing still. The shepherd grows by serving. By going deeper with Allah. By raising men who will one day be shepherds themselves. Your work is never done. But it is no longer only yours.

cta.title: You have done the work. Now lead others through it.
cta.body: Men at your stage are rare. And the men who come after you need what you carry. If you are ready to lead alongside brothers who are at your level, to sharpen each other and build something that outlasts all of us, we are here for that conversation.
